topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammer striking the post was sharp and insistent, a jarring intrusion into the quiet afternoon. I stood by the window, my knuckles white where I gripped the sill, watching Mr. Henderson meticulously measure the length of wood. Every time he spoke, his voice was clipped, a sound that seemed to scrape against my nerves. I could feel a tightening in my chest, a heavy, unwelcome pressure. I had agreed to the boundary line, a compromise that now felt like a betrayal. When he finally paused, wiping a bead of sweat from his brow, I couldn't meet his gaze. I just stared at the perfectly manicured lawn, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over my shoulder, half-expecting him to appear, his shadow stretching long across my porch. The rhythmic sawing of the wood was loud enough to vibrate through the floorboards. Inside, I paced back and forth, my feet shuffling across the worn rug. A cold slickness coated the back of my throat. It wasn't just the fence; it was the feeling of having let him push me to this point. I squeezed my eyes shut for a moment, trying to physically contain the sudden, hot flush creeping up my neck. When I finally forced myself to look out, I saw him finishing a section, and a deep, sinking feeling settled in my stomach, heavy and undeniable.

[story 3]

"Just a little further here, Mrs. Davies," Mr. Henderson called out, his tone implying that my hesitation was an inconvenience. I nodded, a jerky, shallow movement. My hands were clammy; I kept fiddling with the edge of my gardening apron, unable to focus on anything else. The sun felt too bright, exposing every flaw in my composure. I watched him secure the final post, the metal clasp clicking into place with finality. A profound sense of wrongness washed over me, a dull ache behind my ribs. I wanted to retreat, to disappear into the shadows of my own yard, but I remained rooted, watching the new barrier rise between us.
